entries.title: 12 U.S. Code 搂 5531 - Prohibiting unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices (UDAAP)
entries.subtitle: Federal statutory authority establishing the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau's power to regulate unfair, deceptive, and abusive acts or practices in consumer financial services.
entries.abstract: This statutory provision grants the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) authority to prevent covered persons and service providers from engaging in unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices in connection with consumer financial products and services. The statute establishes specific criteria for determining unfairness (substantial consumer injury not reasonably avoidable and not outweighed by countervailing benefits) and abusiveness (material interference with consumer understanding or unreasonable advantage-taking). The provision also mandates CFPB consultation with banking and other federal agencies and includes specific provisions regarding consideration of seasonal income in residential real estate lending.

entries.title: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act
entries.subtitle: An overview of the major provisions of the 2010 financial regulation reform legislation signed into law in July 2010.
entries.publisher: Cornell Law School Securities Law Clinic
entries.abstract: The Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act represents comprehensive federal legislation enacted in July 2010 to reform financial regulations in response to the 2008 financial crisis. The Act comprises sixteen titles addressing financial stability, consumer protection, securities regulation, banking oversight, and derivatives markets. Major provisions establish the Financial Stability Oversight Council, create the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection, implement orderly liquidation authority for systemically important institutions, and impose new requirements on hedge fund advisers, mortgage lending, and derivatives trading.

entries.title: Antitrust Laws
entries.subtitle: An overview of federal antitrust statutes and the legal frameworks for analyzing anticompetitive business practices.
entries.abstract: This page provides a comprehensive overview of the three key federal antitrust statutes: Sherman Act Section 1, Sherman Act Section 2, and the Clayton Act. It explains the distinction between per se violations and rule of reason analysis, the treatment of horizontal versus vertical agreements, and specific prohibited practices including price-fixing, predatory pricing, tying arrangements, and exclusive dealing. The document also covers merger review standards established by the FTC and DOJ, including the 2010 horizontal merger guidelines and 2023 revised merger guidelines that emphasize unilateral and coordination effects analysis.

entries.title: The Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act of 1978 (FISA)
entries.abstract: This document provides a comprehensive summary of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA) of 1978 and its subsequent amendments through 2011. It details the statutory procedures established for electronic surveillance, physical searches, pen registers and trap & trace devices, and business records acquisition in foreign intelligence contexts. The document outlines the creation and role of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court (FISC), differential standards for U.S. persons versus foreign targets, exceptions to warrant requirements, record destruction obligations, and civil rights protections. Major amendments by the USA PATRIOT Act and FISA Amendments Act of 2008 expanded surveillance authorities while the framework maintains judicial and congressional oversight mechanisms.

entries.title: Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Device Software Functions: Lifecycle Management and Marketing Submission Recommendations
entries.abstract: This draft guidance document establishes recommendations for manufacturers submitting marketing applications for medical devices that incorporate artificial intelligence-enabled software functions. It outlines required documentation and information necessary for FDA evaluation of device safety and effectiveness, while proposing design, development, and implementation recommendations for AI-enabled devices. The guidance reflects a comprehensive risk management approach applicable across the device total product life cycle. The document aims to standardize submission contents and support appropriate regulatory assessment of AI-integrated medical technologies.

entries.title: Comments to IL Lawmakers Regarding the Consumer Protection (Group 1) Proposals (SB 3384 / SB 3444)
entries.subtitle: Chamber of Progress submits testimony to the Illinois Subcommittee on AI and Social Media regarding proposed AI liability and consumer protection frameworks.
entries.abstract: This document presents formal comments from the Chamber of Progress, a technology industry association, to the Illinois legislature regarding proposed consumer protection bills addressing artificial intelligence systems. The submission argues that overly broad AI liability frameworks risk creating legal uncertainty and limiting beneficial technology access, while advocating for standards-based approaches with safe harbor provisions as exemplified by California's SB 534 and New York's RAISE Act. The comments contend that existing consumer protection laws and tort principles provide adequate protection without AI-specific liability regimes.

entries.title: FTC Surveillance Pricing 6(b) Study: Research Summaries A Staff Perspective
entries.abstract: This document presents initial findings from the Federal Trade Commission's Surveillance Pricing 6(b) study launched in July 2024, which examines how companies use personal data and algorithmic technologies to implement targeted pricing strategies. The study focuses on intermediary firms that enable algorithmic price discrimination through data collection, analysis, and segmentation. The FTC seeks to understand the types of data fueling these algorithms, their sources, affected industries, and the technical mechanisms enabling surveillance pricing. The document emphasizes this represents preliminary work requiring further research and collaboration.

entries.title: California芒聙聶s SB 53: The First Frontier AI Law, Explained
entries.abstract: California's SB 53, signed by Governor Newsom on September 29, establishes the first state statute specifically targeting frontier artificial intelligence safety and transparency. The law imposes disclosure and transparency obligations on advanced AI developers, including published governance frameworks, pre-deployment transparency reports, critical safety incident reporting mechanisms, and whistleblower protections. This analysis examines SB 53's scope, requirements, and enforcement mechanisms, contrasting its broader catastrophic risk standard with New York's narrower liability framework in the RAISE Act.

entries.title: Forecasting the Economic Effects of AI: Predictions From Economists, AI Experts, and the Public
entries.subtitle: A large-scale forecasting exercise examining expert and public predictions of AI's economic impact through 2050.
entries.publisher: Forecasting Research Institute, Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago, Yale School of Management, Stanford University, University of Pennsylvania
entries.abstract: This working paper presents results from a comprehensive forecasting exercise surveying 69 leading economists, 52 AI industry and policy experts, 38 superforecasters, and 401 members of the general public regarding AI's economic impacts. While respondents predict significant AI capability improvements by 2030, they forecast that key economic indicators including GDP, total factor productivity, and labor force participation will remain close to historical trends. The analysis identifies factors explaining this apparent disconnect, including uneven sectoral diffusion, time-lagged technology adoption, and geopolitical headwinds, and evaluates policy interventions such as job retraining.

entries.title: CFPB Consumer Laws and Regulations: Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices
entries.subtitle: Examination procedures and regulatory guidance for identifying and assessing unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts and practices under the Dodd-Frank Act.
entries.abstract: This document provides regulatory guidance from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) on the standards and examination procedures for identifying unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices (UDAAPs) in consumer financial services. It establishes the three-part legal standard for unfairness derived from the Dodd-Frank Act: substantial injury to consumers, non-avoidability of injury, and injury not outweighed by countervailing benefits. The guidance details principles for assessing institutional compliance and risk, including analysis of consumer decision-making, information asymmetries, and market conditions.

entries.title: EU Artificial Intelligence Act
entries.abstract: The European Union has introduced new legislation on artificial intelligence: The EU AI Act. It lays the foundations for the regulation of AI in the EU.

entries.title: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights
entries.subtitle: A White House framework establishing five principles to guide the design and deployment of automated systems while protecting civil rights and democratic values.
entries.publisher: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy
entries.abstract: The White House Office of Science and Technology Policy proposes a framework of five principles to govern automated systems and artificial intelligence deployment in the United States. The framework addresses documented harms including algorithmic bias in hiring and credit decisions, unsafe medical systems, and unchecked data collection practices that threaten privacy and civil rights. Accompanied by an implementation handbook titled "From Principles to Practice," the framework aims to balance technological innovation with the protection of civil rights, equal opportunity, and foundational American democratic values as directed by presidential executive order.

entries.title: Trump Administration National AI Policy Framework
entries.subtitle: The Trump Administration proposes a comprehensive national legislative framework for artificial intelligence development and deployment across six key policy objectives.
entries.abstract: This policy proposal outlines the Trump Administration's national framework for artificial intelligence governance, organized around six primary objectives: child protection and parental empowerment, community and economic safeguarding, intellectual property rights protection, free speech preservation, innovation enablement, and workforce development. The framework emphasizes federal coordination to establish uniform national standards rather than fragmented state-level regulation, with stated goals of maintaining American competitiveness in AI development while addressing public concerns regarding privacy, security, and societal impacts. The proposal calls for Congressional action to implement legislative measures addressing emerging AI policy challenges including data center permitting, AI-enabled fraud prevention, content moderation safeguards, and intellectual property frameworks.

entries.title: OpenAI Policy Proposal to the Office of Science and Technology Policy
entries.subtitle: OpenAI submits recommendations for the Trump Administration's AI Action Plan emphasizing American leadership, AI education, and economic opportunity.
entries.publisher: Office of Science and Technology Policy / OpenAI
entries.abstract: OpenAI submits policy proposals to the Office of Science and Technology Policy addressing the development of a new AI Action Plan. The submission emphasizes the importance of maintaining American leadership in artificial intelligence development relative to international competitors, particularly the People's Republic of China. The proposal highlights demographic trends in AI adoption among younger populations and argues for increased integration of AI education in schools to prepare students for future economic opportunities.
